title: Mycoplasma pneumoniae–associated Central Nervous System Manifestations: Current Knowledge and Challenges
one_line_summary: Review of 207 cases of M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations from 2015-2025, highlighting encephalitis predominance, diagnostic challenges, and favorable outcomes with antibiotics and immunomodulation.
key_pearls: Encephalitis is the most common CNS manifestation, representing **72%** of cases, with an age range of **3–69 years**.
Diagnosis relies primarily on serum antibody detection (**88.4%** of cases), as PCR in CSF is positive in only **15.5%** of patients.
Antibiotics effective against mycoplasma were used in **88%** of cases, with macrolides (especially azithromycin) being the most common.
Complete recovery was achieved in **81%** of patients; immunomodulatory therapies (steroids, IVIG) were used in **72.5%** of cases.
The prodromal interval between respiratory and neurological symptoms is almost evenly split (<7 vs ≥7 days), suggesting both direct bacterial and autoimmune-mediated pathogenesis.
Macrolide resistance, primarily in East Asia, necessitates consideration of alternative antibiotics like tetracyclines or fluoroquinolones in treatment failures.
population: Patients aged 3-69 years with M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations, predominantly male (56.6%), with the majority in the 0-10 (48.3%) and 11-20 (30.0%) age groups.

sections.heading: Background
sections.content:
- *Mycoplasma pneumoniae* is a common cause of community-acquired respiratory infections, predominantly affecting children and young adults.
- Extrapulmonary manifestations, including CNS diseases, can affect up to **30%** of infected individuals.
- The pathogen lacks a cell wall, making it intrinsically resistant to beta-lactam antibiotics.
- Macrolides are the favored treatment, but resistance is a growing concern, especially in East Asia.
- The COVID-19 pandemic initially caused a drastic decrease in M. pneumoniae incidence, followed by a global resurgence in **2023-2024** after hygiene measures were lifted.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.content:
- English-language publications in PubMed from **January 2015 to October 2025** were reviewed.
- Search terms included combinations of "Mycoplasma pneumoniae" and CNS-related keywords (encephalitis, meningoencephalitis, myelitis, etc.).
- Inclusion criteria: studies with a clear link between CNS manifestation and M. pneumoniae infection, regardless of geographic location.
- Exclusion criteria: studies without definition of infectious causes of neurological manifestation or missing key patient data.
- A total of **70 studies** involving **207 patients** were included; **80%** were case reports, **20%** were case series.

sections.heading: Incidence and Pathogenesis
sections.content:
- CNS complications are estimated to occur in **~0.1%** of all M. pneumoniae infections, with recent studies reporting rates up to **3%**.
- M. pneumoniae-associated encephalitis may represent up to **13%** of all encephalitis cases and is a major etiology of acute pediatric encephalitis.
- Pathogenesis is not fully clear and may involve:
  - Direct bacterial invasion of the CNS with local cytokine induction (IL-18, IL-8, Th-1, Th-2).
  - Indirect immune-mediated damage via autoimmunity, molecular mimicry, or immune complex production.
  - A hypercoagulable state leading to intravascular coagulation and thromboembolism.
- Two clinical patterns are described based on prodrome duration:
  - **Early-onset**: shorter prodrome (<7 days), suggesting direct bacterial involvement.
  - **Late-onset**: prolonged prodrome (≥7 days), suggesting autoimmune-mediated processes.
- Identified autoantibodies include anti-GalC, anti-MOG, anti-IgLON5, anti-NMDAR, anti-GM2, and anti-GQ1b.
section_pearls: The prodromal interval (<7 vs ≥7 days) is a key clinical feature for distinguishing between direct bacterial and autoimmune-mediated pathogenesis.

sections.heading: Results of Literature Review
sections.content:
- **Demographics**: **56.6%** of patients were male; age groups **0-10 years (48.3%)** and **11-20 years (30.0%)** predominated.
- **CNS Manifestations** (Table 1):
  - Encephalitis/meningoencephalitis: **148 cases (71.5%)**, mean age **13.2 years**.
  - MERS: **11 cases**, mean age **19.6 years**.
  - Ischemic stroke/infarction: **11 cases**, mean age **16.5 years**.
  - Cerebellitis/cerebellar ataxia: **8 cases**, mean age **10.0 years**.
  - ADEM: **8 cases**, mean age **17.4 years**.
  - Optic neuritis: **7 cases**, mean age **31.0 years**.
  - Transverse myelitis: **6 cases**, mean age **27.5 years**.
  - Aseptic meningitis: **5 cases**, mean age **11.2 years**.
  - Other (striatal necrosis, vasculitis, rhabdomyolysis): 1 case each.
- **Diagnostic Methods**:
  - Serum antibody detection (IgM/IgG): **88.4%**.
  - PCR from respiratory tract: **42.5%**.
  - Antibody detection in CSF: **35.7%**.
  - PCR from CSF: positive in only **15.5%**.
- **CSF Findings**: Pleocytosis in **58.5%**, elevated protein in **49.7%**; not reliable clinical parameters.
- **Autoantibodies**: Tested in 58 patients, positive in **39.7%**.
- **Prodromal Interval**: In encephalitis cases, almost equally distributed between <7 days (62 cases) and ≥7 days (59 cases).
section_pearls: CSF pleocytosis and elevated protein are not reliable markers for M. pneumoniae CNS involvement.
PCR detection of M. pneumoniae in CSF is rare (15.5%), suggesting a predominantly post-infectious or immune-mediated pathology.

sections.heading: Treatment and Outcome
sections.content:
- **Antibiotic Therapy**:
  - Used in **87.9%** of cases.
  - Macrolides alone in **80.2%** (azithromycin **76.5%**, clarithromycin **7.2%**, erythromycin **2.4%**).
  - Tetracyclines or fluoroquinolones used exclusively in 21 patients, including 9 children aged **4-10 years** without reported side effects.
  - Macrolide dose: typically **500 mg/day** for adults, **10 mg/kg/day** for children.
  - Treatment duration: **3 to 15 days** (macrolides mostly **7-10 days**).
- **Immunomodulatory Therapy**:
  - Used in **72.5%** of patients.
  - Steroids alone: **32.4%**.
  - IVIG alone: **23.2%**.
  - IVIG + steroids: **16.9%**.
  - Plasmapheresis: 4 cases each in combination with steroids or steroids + IVIG.
  - One study confirmed **significantly** shorter duration of clinical improvement and hospitalization with macrolides combined with IVIG or steroids.
- **Outcome**:
  - Complete recovery: **81.1%**.
  - Mild deficits: **4.8%**.
  - Poor outcome (severe neurological dysfunction, low GCS, vision loss): **13.1%**.
  - Death: 2 patients (aged 37 and 58) with severe preexisting conditions, from ischemic stroke and hemorrhagic leukoencephalitis.
section_pearls: Early antibiotic treatment is emphasized for favorable outcomes, though no case-control studies prove its benefit.
In regions with high macrolide resistance, treatment should be switched to tetracyclines or fluoroquinolones if macrolides fail.

sections.heading: Current Challenges: Diagnosis
sections.content:
- **Diagnostic Limitations**:
  - PCR from respiratory tract: high sensitivity/specificity but can be positive in asymptomatic carriers and negative in late stages.
  - PCR from CSF: if positive, clear indication of involvement, but kits are not validated for CSF and it is often negative in autoimmune-mediated cases.
  - Serodiagnosis (serum): simple sampling, but IgM may be negative in early infection (≤7 days) or after re-infection in adults; long-term antibody presence after acute infection.
  - Serodiagnosis (CSF): kits not validated for CSF; results depend on blood-brain barrier integrity.
  - Antibody index (serum/CSF): confirms intrathecal antibody production but requires nearly simultaneous sampling and additional parameters (albumin, total immunoglobulins).
- **Diagnostic Classification**:
  - *Possible*: single elevated specific antibodies in serum and/or PCR detection in respiratory tract.
  - *Probable*: 4-fold specific antibody increase in serum.
  - *Confirmed*: M. pneumoniae detection in CSF or specific intrathecal antibody response.
- The combination of serological and molecular methods for respiratory samples, serum, and CSF is considered optimal but often not feasible.
section_pearls: Commercially available kits for serum antibody or respiratory PCR detection are not validated for CSF, posing regulatory and quality management challenges.
A confirmed diagnosis of M. pneumoniae CNS involvement was achieved in only a few cases across the reviewed studies.

sections.heading: Current Challenges: Clinical and Epidemiological Aspects
sections.content:
- Respiratory symptoms of M. pneumoniae are not clearly distinguishable from other community-acquired pathogens; coinfections are common.
- Asymptomatic carriers and mild, inconspicuous infections can precede neurological manifestations, complicating differential diagnosis.
- Treatment is not based on a uniform regimen; antibiotics are often initiated only after diagnostic confirmation, potentially delaying bacterial eradication.
- Cases without antibiotic use (9.7%) did not appear to have a more severe course, and treatment failures may occur due to poor CNS penetration or autoimmune-mediated pathology.
- Macrolide resistance (mainly 23S rRNA mutations in East Asia) is a concern; a meta-analysis confirmed increased extrapulmonary complications with resistant strains.
- Strain typing (P1 types) may influence host immune response, but studies on CNS complications are lacking.
- Despite a global surge in M. pneumoniae infections in 2023-2024, a noticeable increase in CNS cases was rarely reported, possibly due to underdetection.
section_pearls: Clinicians should include M. pneumoniae in the differential diagnosis of infection-related CNS symptoms and initiate appropriate diagnostics and therapy.
The influence of M. pneumoniae subtypes on the development and course of neurological manifestations is currently unknown.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.content:
- M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations remain a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge.
- Encephalitis is the most common CNS complication and carries a risk of severe neurological sequelae.
- A combination of pathomechanisms, coinfections, strain characteristics, and patient immune status contributes to the diverse disease spectrum.
- A comprehensive diagnostic approach is necessary, and validation of serological/molecular tests for CSF would benefit clinical practice.
- Early, targeted therapy including antibiotics effective against mycoplasma and, depending on severity, immunomodulators leads to favorable outcomes in most patients.
- Uncertainty remains regarding the practical efficacy of antimicrobial and immunomodulatory treatments, necessitating further studies to establish standardized therapy guidelines.

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: Comprehensive review of recent literature (2015-2025) with a large case series (207 patients); detailed analysis of diagnostic methods, treatment patterns, and outcomes; addresses both clinical and epidemiological challenges.
- Limitations: Only PubMed-indexed studies were included, likely leading to underreporting of mild cases; potential publication bias toward more severe or complex cases; quantitative comparisons between CNS complication types are not possible without long-term case-control studies; the COVID-19 pandemic period may have reduced the number of reported cases.

drugs_doses.indication: First-line macrolide for M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Macrolide resistance (23S rRNA mutations) common in East Asia; generally well-tolerated

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; used in 7.2% of macrolide-treated cases
drugs_doses.indication: Alternative macrolide for M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; used in 2.4% of macrolide-treated cases
drugs_doses.indication: Alternative macrolide for M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Macrolide resistance; gastrointestinal side effects more common

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; used in 21 patients including children aged 4-10 years
drugs_doses.indication: Tetracycline alternative for macrolide-resistant or refractory M. pneumoniae CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Generally contraindicated in children <8 years due to teeth discoloration and bone growth inhibition; no side effects reported in this cohort

drugs_doses.indication: Tetracycline alternative for macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Similar to doxycycline; CNS penetration may be better

drugs_doses.indication: Fluoroquinolone alternative for macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Tendinopathy, CNS effects; generally avoided in children

drugs_doses.indication: Fluoroquinolone alternative for macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Tendinopathy, CNS effects; generally avoided in children

drugs_doses.indication: Fluoroquinolone alternative for macrolide-resistant M. pneumoniae CNS manifestations
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Tendinopathy, CNS effects; generally avoided in children

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; used in 23.2% of patients alone, 16.9% in combination with steroids
drugs_doses.indication: Immunomodulatory therapy for M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations, especially autoimmune-mediated cases
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Thrombotic events, renal impairment, anaphylaxis; generally well-tolerated

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; used in 32.4% of patients alone, 16.9% in combination with IVIG
drugs_doses.indication: Immunomodulatory therapy for M. pneumoniae-associated CNS manifestations; evidence shows shortened disease duration in severe respiratory infections
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Hyperglycemia, immunosuppression, psychiatric effects; early use recommended to minimize neuronal damage
